  "findings": [
    "Slings are routed over unprotected edges, creating a point of contact that can abrade, cut, or weaken sling fibers or wires during the lift.",
    "Unprotected edges fail to provide the edge protection or padding required to prevent sling damage and possible sudden failure under load.",
    "Configuration is inconsistent with safe rigging practice because the sling load-bearing contact is against a sharp or unguarded structural edge."
  ],
  "actions": [
    "Stop the lift and remove load from the slings.",
    "Install rated edge protectors, saddles, or padding at all contact points between slings and structural edges.",
    "Inspect slings for abrasion, cuts, broken wires, or fiber damage; replace if any damage is found.",
    "Re-rig with protected edges and verify sling angles and capacities before lifting.",
    "Have a competent person re-inspect the rigging setup before resuming operations."
  ],
  "rationale": "ASME B30 and OSHA 1926.251 require slings to be protected from sharp edges and abrasive surfaces that could damage them during a lift. Slings over unprotected edges create a high-risk condition for sling damage and sudden failure, making the configuration non-compliant and unsafe. Because the work is rough-complete, the rigging is installed but not yet closed out, so the defect must be corrected before acceptance or use.",
  "references": [
    "ASME B30.9 (Slings) - protection of slings from edges and abrasive surfaces",
    "ASME B30.10 / B30.20 - rigging hardware and lifting practices",
    "OSHA 1926.251(a)(3) - slings shall not be loaded in excess of rated capacity and shall be protected from sharp edges"
  ]
}
